XtODT3.tier2: Crime & Harmful Acts to Individuals, Society & Human Right Violations

content_desc.clip_title: Kevin's Ultimate Choice and Sacrifice
content_desc.summary_paragraph: Milton reveals his grand apocalyptic plan, urging Kevin to father the Antichrist with his half-sister, Christabella. As Christabella attempts to seduce him and Milton offers him ultimate power and pleasure without consequence, Kevin grapples with his destiny. Asserting his ultimate free will, Kevin abruptly shoots himself in the head to thwart the prophecy, sending Milton into a fiery, agonizing rage as the room is engulfed in hellfire.

key_actions: Milton pitching his apocalyptic plan to Kevin
Kevin abruptly shooting himself in the head
Milton screaming in agony as his plan fails
The room transforming into a fiery hellscape
dialogue_highlights: Free will... it is a bitch.
What about love? Overrated. Biochemically no different than eating large quantities of chocolate.
Free will, right?

trivia.text: As the screenplay developed, free-will became a theme in which Milton does not actually cause events.
trivia.match_reasoning: Kevin explicitly mentions "free will" before shooting himself, an action that Milton clearly does not cause or desire.

trivia.text: The screenwriters added the plot element that Lomax was Milton's son, and that Milton could produce the Antichrist, neither of which are in the novel.
trivia.match_reasoning: Milton refers to Kevin as his son and reveals his plan for Kevin to father the Antichrist.

trivia.text: Pacino came up with the idea of dancing to "It Happened in Monterey" by Frank Sinatra, and Hackford immediately adopted the idea.
trivia.match_reasoning: Pacino's character dances to the song "It Happened in Monterey" during the scene.

trivia.text: At the end of the film, John Milton morphs into Lucifer as a fallen angel.
trivia.match_reasoning: At the end of the clip, Milton morphs into a fallen angel with wings.

trivia.text: The crew created the effect by combining life masks depicting Reeves, Pacino in 1997 and Pacino as he appeared in the 1972 film The Godfather.
trivia.match_reasoning: The visual effect of Milton's face morphing into different faces is shown right before he turns into a fallen angel.

trivia.text: Shots of ballerinas moving in water were used as a basis for Milton's animated sculpture.
trivia.match_reasoning: The animated sculpture on the wall behind the characters comes to life and moves during the scene.

trivia.text: Williams said that he filmed the people for the statue effect, and that they were naked and placed in a tank next to a blue screen.
trivia.match_reasoning: The animated statue effect, featuring naked figures moving, is prominently featured on the wall in Milton's apartment.

trivia.text: It took three months to film the people and add the computer effects, at a cost of $2 million, which was 40% of the overall budget for special effects.
trivia.match_reasoning: The computer-generated animated statue effect discussed in this fact is visible throughout the scene.

trivia.text: Hackford dubbed Pacino's performance of "It Happened in Monterey" with Sinatra's voice.
trivia.match_reasoning: Frank Sinatra's voice is heard singing "It Happened in Monterey" as Pacino's character dances to it.

scenes.summary: Kevin suddenly finds himself back in the courthouse bathroom in Florida, realizing his entire experience in New York was a vision. He washes his face and leaves his wedding ring on the sink.

content_desc.clip_title: Tense Moment in the Courthouse Restroom
content_desc.summary_paragraph: A distressed Kevin Lomax stands in a courthouse restroom, splashing water on his face to calm down. A reporter exits a stall and offers some casual words of consolation about his winning streak ending. After the reporter leaves, Kevin stares intensely at his reflection before picking up a wedding ring resting on the sink counter.

dialogue_highlights: It's a nice run, Kev.
Nobody wins 'em all.

scenes.summary: Kevin returns to the courtroom and reunites with a living Mary Ann. When the trial resumes, Kevin shocks the court by announcing he can no longer represent his guilty client, risking disbarment to do the right thing.

content_desc.clip_title: Lawyer Abruptly Resigns From Case in Crowded Courtroom
content_desc.summary_paragraph: A defense attorney enters a busy courtroom and shares a tender kiss with his wife before the trial resumes. When the judge calls on him to question the young female witness, he hesitates and shockingly announces he can no longer represent his client. The courtroom erupts in chaos as the judge demands a meeting in his chambers and the client angrily threatens the lawyer. Amidst the uproar, the lawyer embraces his wife, telling her he believes he finally did the right thing.

characters: Kevin Lomax (Defense Attorney) - conflicted then resolute
Mary Ann Lomax (Wife) - loving and supportive
Judge Garson Deeds - authoritative and outraged
key_actions: Standing up to address the judge
dialogue_highlights: I can no longer represent my client. I need to be replaced as counsel.
You just made the biggest mistake of your life.
The right thing. I think the right thing.

trivia.text: For court scenes, New Jersey's Bergen County Court House was employed for production, as were historic courthouses in New York.
trivia.match_reasoning: The entirety of this clip takes place during a court scene, which were filmed at the Bergen County Court House and historic courthouses in New York.

scenes.summary: Reporters swarm Kevin as he leaves the courtroom. Larry the reporter convinces Kevin to give him an exclusive interview, appealing to his ego. After Kevin agrees and walks away, Larry transforms into Milton, remarking that vanity is his favorite sin.

content_desc.clip_title: Kevin Agrees to an Interview as Milton Reveals Himself
content_desc.summary_paragraph: Kevin and Mary Ann Lomax exit a courtroom and are immediately swarmed by aggressive reporters. A journalist named Larry persistently pitches Kevin on an exclusive story about his sudden crisis of conscience. Tempted by the promise of stardom, Kevin agrees to call him the next morning. As the couple walks away, Larry's face morphs into John Milton, who breaks the fourth wall to declare that vanity is his favorite sin before the scene bursts into flames.

dialogue_highlights: This is a story that needs to be told. It's you. You're a star.
Vanity... definitely my favorite sin.

trivia.text: "Paint It Black" by The Rolling Stones is also used for the film's conclusion.
trivia.match_reasoning: The distinctive intro to 'Paint It Black' by The Rolling Stones begins playing at the end of the clip after Milton delivers his final line.

scenes.summary: The end credits roll over a background of flames.
The end credits roll.
